verses.commentary: Warning to the rich. Weep and wail because of misery coming upon you. Prophetic judgment. Wealth accumulated unjustly will not save. Judgment is certain for oppressors. The rich here are unbelievers who exploit others.
Your wealth has rotted, moths have eaten your clothes. Images of decay. Stored wealth is temporary and corruptible. Material accumulation is futile. What seems secure is already destroyed.

Gold and silver are corroded. Their corrosion testifies against you and will eat your flesh like fire. You have hoarded wealth in the last days. Even precious metals corrode. Wealth meant to be used is hoarded. Hoarding in the end times is tragic folly.

The wages you failed to pay workers cry out. The cries of harvesters have reached the ears of the Lord Almighty (Kyrios Sabaōth, Κύριος Σαβαώθ). Oppression of workers is heard by God. Withheld wages testify against oppressors. God is the defender of the exploited.

You have lived in luxury and self-indulgence. You have fattened yourselves in the day of slaughter. Like cattle fattened for slaughter, the rich are unknowingly preparing for judgment. Indulgence blinds to impending destruction.
Judicial murder or economic oppression that leads to death. The innocent suffer while the guilty prosper, but not forever.

Be patient, brothers and sisters, until the Lord's coming. Transition from judgment on the rich to exhortation for believers. Patience (makrothymeō, μακροθυμέω): long-suffering, endurance. The farmer waits for the crop, patiently expecting autumn and spring rains. Agricultural patience as metaphor for eschatological hope.
You too, be patient and stand firm. The Lord's coming is near. Stand firm (stērizō, στηρίζω): establish, strengthen. Patience is active, not passive. Nearness of Christ's return fuels perseverance.

Do not grumble against one another, or you will be judged. The Judge is standing at the door. Imminence of judgment should prevent internal conflict. Grumbling under pressure is sin. Christ's return is imminent; live accordingly.

Take the prophets who spoke in the Lord's name as examples of patience in suffering. Old Testament prophets suffered yet remained faithful. Their endurance is a model for believers. Suffering is not evidence of God's absence but part of the prophetic calling.

We count as blessed those who have persevered. You have heard of Job's perseverance and seen what the Lord finally brought about. The Lord is full of compassion and mercy. Job is the exemplar of patient endurance. God's purpose in suffering is ultimately good. Suffering is temporary; God's mercy is eternal.

Above all, do not swear by heaven, earth, or anything else. Let your yes be yes, and your no be no. Otherwise you will be condemned. Echoes Jesus' teaching (Matthew 5:33-37). Oaths indicate distrust. Simple, honest speech should suffice. Integrity makes oaths unnecessary.

Is anyone in trouble? Pray. Is anyone happy? Sing songs of praise. Prayer and praise are appropriate responses to all circumstances. Trouble drives us to God; joy overflows in worship. Life circumstances should lead to God.

Is anyone sick? Call the elders to pray and anoint with oil in the Lord's name. Corporate prayer for the sick. Anointing with oil may be medicinal or symbolic. Church leadership involved in care for the sick. Prayer is the primary action.

The prayer offered in faith will make the sick person well. The Lord will raise them up. If they have sinned, they will be forgiven. Faith-filled prayer leads to healing and forgiveness. Sickness and sin are sometimes connected, but not always. God is sovereign in healing.

Confess your sins to each other and pray for each other so you may be healed. The prayer of a righteous person is powerful and effective (energoumenē, ἐνεργουμένη: actively working). Mutual confession and intercession. Prayer is not passive but dynamic, effective. Righteousness enables powerful prayer.

Elijah was a human being like us. He prayed earnestly that it would not rain, and it did not rain for three and a half years. Elijah as example of effective prayer. He was ordinary, yet his prayers had extraordinary power. Faithfulness, not superhuman ability, makes prayer effective.

Again he prayed, and the heavens gave rain, and the earth produced crops. Elijah's prayers controlled the weather (1 Kings 17-18). God responds to the prayers of the faithful. Prayer is powerful because God is powerful. The righteous have access to divine power through prayer.

If one of you should wander from the truth and someone should bring that person back. Restoration is communal responsibility. Wandering from truth is spiritual danger. The church must pursue straying members. Truth is not subjective; it is fixed, and one can wander from it.

Whoever turns a sinner from the error of their way will save them from death and cover over a multitude of sins. Restoration saves from eternal death. Covering sins may mean divine forgiveness or reconciliation within the community. Evangelism and discipleship are acts of love that save lives.
